title: Bias surface
text: Real-world biodiversity data rarely collected systematically. Biologists often sample near roads, rivers, research stations, creating spatial clustering necessarily reflect optimal habitat. nicheR package allows simulate spatial sampling biases. workflow involves two steps: prepare_bias(): Standardizing raw environmental anthropogenic covariates probability-scaling surfaces. apply_bias(): Mathematically fusing bias surfaces habitat suitability predictions.

title: Bias surface
text: Raw bias proxies (e.g., species richness, nighttime lights, distance roads) come varying scales units. prepare_bias() resamples, aligns, min-max standardizes layers strict [0, 1] scale. multiple layers provided, allows assign unique directional effects multiplying single composite surface.

title: Bias surface
text: bias_surface: SpatRaster (single multi-layer) list SpatRaster objects representing raw bias proxies. effect_direction: Character vector dictating raw values map sampling probability: "direct": Higher raw values = higher sampling probability (kept ). "inverse": Higher raw values = lower sampling probability (transformed 1 - x). Note: providing multi-layer raster, can pass vector directions mapping respective layer. template_layer: Optional SpatRaster. reference layer used align bias layers (matching resolution, extent, CRS). NULL, algorithm automatically selects finest-resolution layer input. include_composite: Logical. TRUE (default), function outputs combined, multiplied composite bias surface. include_processed_layers: Logical. TRUE, includes standardized individual layers multiplied together. Default FALSE. mask_na: Logical. Controls NA (NoData) pixels handled across multiple layers: TRUE (Intersection): pixel NA layer, final composite pixel becomes NA. FALSE (Union - default): Ignores NAs layers valid values, preserving much spatial coverage possible. verbose: Logical. TRUE, prints processing steps console.

title: Bias surface
text: example, raw_bias raster contains two layers: sp_richness nighttime (nighttime lights/urbanization). Ecologically, sampling effort likely highest areas known high species richness, lowest highly urbanized areas. Therefore, assign "direct" effect richness "inverse" effect nighttime lights create realistic composite bias surface.  Notice final composite surface creates “hotspots” sampling areas feature high species richness low urbanization, accurately reflecting combined effect_direction parameter.
code: library(nicheR) library(terra) #> terra 1.9.11  # Load a sample bias layer containing 'sp_richness' and 'nighttime' biases_file <- system.file("extdata", "ma_biases.tif", package = "nicheR") raw_bias <- rast(biases_file)  # Prepare a composite bias surface mapping unique directions to each layer prep_composite <- prepare_bias(bias_surface = raw_bias,                                 effect_direction = c("direct", "inverse"),                                 verbose = FALSE)  # --- Plotting the Output --- par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 2))  # Plot the raw inputs plot(raw_bias[["sp_richness"]], main = "Species Richness") plot(raw_bias[["nighttime"]], main = "Nighttime Lights")  # Plot the resulting unified bias probability surface plot(prep_composite$composite_surface, main = "Composite Bias Surface") dev.off() #> null device  #>           1

title: Bias surface
text: bias surface scaled 0 1, must integrate actual ecological prediction (e.g., habitat suitability). apply_bias() aligns grids multiplies suitability bias. resulting output weighted surface, true biological probability.

title: Bias surface
text: prepared_bias: single-layer SpatRaster, list output generated prepare_bias(). prediction: SpatRaster containing suitability predictions. Values must within [0, 1]. prediction_layer: Character. prediction raster multiple layers, specify exact name layer use (e.g., "suitability"). NULL, defaults first layer. effect_direction: Character. Dictates prepared bias applied suitability: "direct" (default): Multiplies suitability ×\times bias. "inverse": Multiplies suitability ×\times(1−bias)(1 - \text{bias}). verbose: Logical. TRUE, prints processing steps console.

title: Bias surface
text: , take standard suitability prediction restrict using composite bias layer just prepared. apply "direct" multiplier, meaning areas high composite bias scores retain suitability, areas low bias scores penalized.  final comparison, observe spatial footprint species shrinks based bias layer. drawing points applied_bias raster using sample_biased_data(), algorithm forced ignore large swaths highly suitable habitat simply simulated sampling effort (driven nighttime lights richness) areas low.
code: # (Assuming 'pred' is a previously generated suitability raster from build_ellipsoid) # Let's generate a quick prediction for this example bios_file <- system.file("extdata", "ma_bios.tif", package = "nicheR") bios <- rast(bios_file) range_df <- data.frame(bio_1 = c(22, 28), bio_12 = c(1000, 3500), bio_15 = c(50, 70)) ell <- build_ellipsoid(range = range_df) #> Starting: building ellipsoidal niche from ranges... #> Step: computing covariance matrix... #> Step: computing additional ellipsoidal niche metrics... #> Done: created ellipsoidal niche. pred <- predict(ell, newdata = bios, include_suitability = TRUE) #> Starting: suitability prediction using newdata of class: SpatRaster... #> Step: Ignoring extra predictor columns: bio_5, bio_6, bio_7, bio_13, bio_14 #> Step: Using 3 predictor variables: bio_1, bio_12, bio_15 #> Done: Prediction completed successfully. Returned raster layers: Mahalanobis, suitability  # Apply the composite bias to our suitability layer applied_bias <- apply_bias(prepared_bias = prep_composite,                             prediction = pred,                             prediction_layer = "suitability",                             effect_direction = "direct",                            verbose = FALSE)  # --- Plotting the Output --- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 2))  # Original Biological Suitability plot(pred[["suitability"]], main = "Habitat Suitability")  # Suitability mathematically restricted by our composite sampling bias plot(applied_bias[[1]], main = "Suitability + Composite Bias") dev.off() #> null device  #>           1

text: vignette introduces core workflow defining species ecological niches ellipsoids environmental space using nicheR package. ellipsoid-based approach follows Hutchinsonian concept ecological niche representing set environmental conditions species can persist n-dimensional hypervolume multivariate environmental space. Ellipsoids well-suited task straightforward interpatable way capturing shape, size, orientation species’ niche, including correlations environmental variables. main functions covered vignette : build_ellipsoid(): constructs nicheR_ellipsoid object user-defined environmental ranges. update_ellipsoid_covariance(): modifies covariance structure existing ellipsoid introduce correlations environmental variables. plot_ellipsoid(): visualizes ellipsoid two-dimensional environmental space, optionally alongside background data. add_ellipsoid(): adds additional ellipsoid existing plot visual comparison. add_data(): overlays additional data points (centroids, background data, occurrence records) onto existing ellipsoid plot. plot_ellipsoid_pairs(): plots pairwise projections multidimensional ellipsoid . save_nicheR() / read_nicheR(): saves reloads nicheR objects disk.

title: Creating Ellipsoid Based Niches
text: key distinction ecological niche modeling environmental space (E-space) geographic space (G-space). E-space multivariate space defined environmental variables, temperature precipitation, axis represents single variable point represents unique combination environmental conditions. ellipsoid niche defined parameterized entirely E-space, shape, size, position direct ecological meaning. G-space, contrast, familiar two-dimensional geographic map, point corresponds location landscape. every geographic location can characterized environmental conditions, direct relationship two concepts niche defined E-space can projected onto G-space identify locations real landscape fall within species’ tolerances. projection E-space G-space produces map predicted suitable habitat, covered Making Predictions vignette. foundation niche modeling nicheR ellipsoid, represents set environmental conditions species can tolerate. ellipsoid environmental space defined three components: centroid (optimal central environmental conditions), covariance matrix (controls shape orientation ellipsoid), volume (determined size environmental ranges). boundary ellipsoid defined threshold Mahalanobis distance centroid, multivariate measure distance accounts correlations among variables. Mahalanobis distance point E-space measures far point lies centroid ellipsoid, scaled covariance structure niche. Unlike Euclidean distance, treats directions equally, Mahalanobis distance accounts fact ellipsoid may extend along environmental axes others, axes may correlated. point falls exactly boundary ellipsoid Mahalanobis distance equal chi-square cutoff; points inside ellipsoid smaller distances considered suitable, points outside larger distances considered unsuitable. means suitability declines continuously symmetrically centroid outward, reaching zero ellipsoid boundary.

title: Creating Ellipsoid Based Niches
text: important property ellipsoidal niche covariance environmental variables. Covariance captures idea species may independently tolerate full range variable; instead, tolerance along one axis may depend conditions along another. example, species might tolerate high temperatures precipitation also high, creating positive covariance temperature precipitation. ellipsoid first created build_ellipsoid(), covariances set zero, meaning ellipsoid axes aligned coordinate axes. ellipsoid object contains cov_limits element reports minimum maximum covariance values valid ellipsoid given shape size.  update_ellipsoid_covariance() function applies new covariance value existing ellipsoid. properties object (covariance matrix, ellipsoid boundary, volume) recalculated reflect updated shape. Applying covariance value introduces tilt ellipsoid, reflecting correlation two variables.  Attempting set covariance value outside allowable range return error rather producing invalid ellipsoid.  can use add_ellipsoid() overlay updated ellipsoid plot original, allowing direct visual comparison two shapes. updated ellipsoid (green) shows clear tilt relative original (purple), reflecting introduced covariance.

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: vignette demonstrates plotting functions available nicheR visualizing ellipsoidal niche models environmental space (E-space). plots operate E-space: axes represent environmental variables points represent environmental combinations background data predictions. functions covered : plot_ellipsoid(): opens new E-space plot showing ellipsoid boundary, optionally background points prediction surface. add_data(): overlays points onto existing plot. Points can single color colored continuous variable suitability. add_ellipsoid(): overlays ellipsoid boundary onto existing plot. plot_ellipsoid_pairs(): produces multi-panel layout showing pairwise two-dimensional projections ellipsoid, globally consistent axis limits. functions designed layered. typical workflow open plot plot_ellipsoid() add data boundaries top add_data() add_ellipsoid().

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: nicheR installed yet, please . See Main guide installation instructions.
code: library(nicheR)  # Saving original plotting parameters original_par <- par(no.readonly = TRUE)

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: use example data vignettes: two-dimensional nicheR_ellipsoid object reference species corresponding North American background data.  also need predictions demonstrate prediction-based plotting modes. compute available throughout vignette.
code: data("ref_ellipse", package = "nicheR") data("back_data",   package = "nicheR") # Non-truncated Mahalanobis distance pred_maha <- predict(ref_ellipse,                      newdata = back_data[, ref_ellipse$var_names],                      include_mahalanobis = TRUE,                      include_suitability = FALSE,                      verbose = FALSE)  # Non-truncated suitability pred_suit <- predict(ref_ellipse,                      newdata = back_data[, ref_ellipse$var_names],                      include_mahalanobis = FALSE,                      include_suitability = TRUE,                      verbose = FALSE)  # Truncated suitability (outside = 0, inside = suitability value) pred_trunc <- predict(ref_ellipse,                       newdata = back_data[, ref_ellipse$var_names],                       include_mahalanobis = FALSE,                       include_suitability = FALSE,                       suitability_truncated = TRUE,                       verbose = FALSE)

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: plot_ellipsoid() opens new E-space plot. three display modes depending provided: Ellipsoid : neither background prediction given. function draws just ellipsoid boundary, sized boundary . starting point building layered plots manually add_data() add_ellipsoid(). Background: background data frame given. Background points drawn first, ellipsoid boundary drawn top, axis limits span . Prediction: prediction data frame given. col_layer also specified, points colored column axis limits span full prediction extent.

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: neither background prediction given, plot_ellipsoid() draws ellipsoid boundary. plot sized boundary extent, axis limits reflect ellipsoid dimensions. mode useful want full manual control goes plot.
code: par(mar = mars)  plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                col_ell = "#e10000",                lwd = 2,                xlab = "Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature)",                ylab = "Bio12 (Annual Precipitation)",                main = "Ellipsoid boundary only")

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: Passing background data frame draws background points first ellipsoid boundary top. Axis limits computed background extent ellipsoid boundary, ellipsoid never clipped.
code: par(mar = mars)  plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                background = back_data[, ref_ellipse$var_names],                col_ell = "#e10000",                col_bg  = "#9a9797",                lwd = 2, pch = ".",                xlab = "Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature)",                ylab = "Bio12 (Annual Precipitation)",                main = "Background points")

what: Prediction colored by a continuous variable
title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: Passing prediction data frame specifying col_layer colors points values column. column prediction data frame can used, including Mahalanobis distance, suitability, truncated versions either. non-truncated predictions every point receives color palette, every point finite positive value. ellipsoid boundary drawn top col_ell.
code: par(mar = mars)  plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                prediction = pred_maha,                col_layer  = "Mahalanobis",                pal        = blue_pal,                col_ell    = "#e10000",                lwd = 2, pch = 16, cex_bg = 0.5,                xlab = "Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature)",                ylab = "Bio12 (Annual Precipitation)",                main = "Mahalanobis distance (non-truncated)")  legend("topright",        legend = c("Ellipsoid boundary", "Low D\u00b2", "High D\u00b2"),        col    = c("#e10000", blue_pal[5], blue_pal[90]),        pch    = c(NA, 16, 16), lty = c(1, NA, NA),        lwd    = c(2, NA, NA), cex = 0.8, bty = "n") par(mar = mars)  plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                prediction = pred_suit,                col_layer  = "suitability",                pal        = vir_pal,                col_ell    = "#e10000",                lwd = 2, pch = 16, cex_bg = 0.5,                xlab = "Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature)",                ylab = "Bio12 (Annual Precipitation)",                main = "Suitability (non-truncated)")  legend("topright",        legend = c("Ellipsoid boundary", "Low suitability", "High suitability"),        col    = c("#e10000", vir_pal[5], vir_pal[95]),        pch    = c(NA, 16, 16), lty = c(1, NA, NA),        lwd    = c(2, NA, NA), cex = 0.8, bty = "n")

what: Truncated predictions and grey outside region
title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: col_layer points truncated prediction column (suitability_trunc Mahalanobis_trunc), plot_ellipsoid() automatically detects outside points, value zero NA, draws col_bg. Inside points drawn top palette color. Axis limits computed full prediction extent view collapse ellipsoid interior.   pattern applies truncated Mahalanobis distance, outside points receive NA drawn col_bg.
code: par(mar = mars)  plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                prediction = pred_trunc,                col_layer  = "suitability_trunc",                pal        = vir_pal,                col_bg     = "#d4d4d4",                col_ell    = "#e10000",                lwd = 2, pch = 16, cex_bg = 0.5,                xlab = "Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature)",                ylab = "Bio12 (Annual Precipitation)",                main = "Truncated suitability")  legend("topright",        legend = c("Ellipsoid boundary", "Low suitability",                   "High suitability", "Outside (zero)"),        col    = c("#e10000", vir_pal[5], vir_pal[95], "#d4d4d4"),        pch    = c(NA, 16, 16, 16), lty = c(1, NA, NA, NA),        lwd    = c(2, NA, NA, NA), cex = 0.8, bty = "n") pred_trunc_maha <- predict(ref_ellipse,                            newdata = back_data[, ref_ellipse$var_names],                            include_mahalanobis   = FALSE,                            include_suitability   = FALSE,                            mahalanobis_truncated = TRUE,                            verbose = FALSE)  par(mar = mars)  plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                prediction = pred_trunc_maha,                col_layer  = "Mahalanobis_trunc",                pal        = blue_pal,                col_bg     = "#d4d4d4",                col_ell    = "#e10000",                lwd = 2, pch = 16, cex_bg = 0.5,                xlab = "Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature)",                ylab = "Bio12 (Annual Precipitation)",                main = "Truncated Mahalanobis distance")  legend("topright",        legend = c("Ellipsoid boundary", "Inside (low D\u00b2)",                   "Inside (high D\u00b2)", "Outside (NA)"),        col    = c("#e10000", blue_pal[5], blue_pal[90], "#d4d4d4"),        pch    = c(NA, 16, 16, 16), lty = c(1, NA, NA, NA),        lwd    = c(2, NA, NA, NA), cex = 0.8, bty = "n")

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: rev_pal = TRUE reverses color palette high values map dark end instead bright end. alpha_bg controls transparency data points alpha_ell controls transparency ellipsoid boundary line, 0 1 scale.
code: par(mar = mars)  plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                prediction = pred_suit,                col_layer  = "suitability",                pal        = vir_pal,                rev_pal    = TRUE,                alpha_bg   = 0.5,                alpha_ell  = 0.8,                col_ell    = "#0004d5",                lwd = 2, pch = 16, cex_bg = 0.5,                xlab = "Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature)",                ylab = "Bio12 (Annual Precipitation)",                main = "Reversed palette + transparency")  legend("topright",        legend = c("Ellipsoid boundary",                   "Low suitability (now bright)",                   "High suitability (now dark)"),        col    = c("#0004d5", vir_pal[95], vir_pal[5]),        pch    = c(NA, 16, 16), lty = c(1, NA, NA),        lwd    = c(2, NA, NA), cex = 0.8, bty = "n")

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: bg_sample argument draws random subsample plotting. useful plotting hundreds thousands background points slow rendering without adding visual information. subsample drawn independently inside outside points col_layer active, groups remain represented.
code: par(mfrow = c(1, 2), cex = 0.75, mar = mars)  plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                background = back_data[, ref_ellipse$var_names],                col_ell = "#e10000", col_bg = "#9a9797",                lwd = 2, pch = ".",                xlab = "Bio1", ylab = "Bio12",                main = "Full background")  plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                background = back_data[, ref_ellipse$var_names],                bg_sample  = 500,                col_ell = "#e10000", col_bg = "#9a9797",                lwd = 2, pch = 16, cex_bg = 0.4,                xlab = "Bio1", ylab = "Bio12",                main = "Subsampled (n = 500)")

what: Fixed axis limits for comparing ellipsoids
title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: fixed_lims argument accepts named list xlim ylim vectors overrides automatically computed axis limits. useful placing two ellipsoids side side wanting panels scale sizes visually comparable.   Without fixed_lims, panel rescale data extent, making two ellipsoids appear size regardless actual difference position spread.
code: # Create a second ellipsoid with a shifted centroid for comparison ref_ellipse2          <- ref_ellipse ref_ellipse2$centroid <- ref_ellipse$centroid + c(5, 100)  # Define shared limits from the full background extent global_lims <- list(   xlim = range(back_data$bio_1,  na.rm = TRUE),   ylim = range(back_data$bio_12, na.rm = TRUE) )  par(mfrow = c(1, 2), cex = 0.75, mar = mars)  plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                background = back_data[, ref_ellipse$var_names],                fixed_lims = global_lims,                col_ell = "#e10000", col_bg = "#9a9797",                lwd = 2, pch = ".",                xlab = "Bio1", ylab = "Bio12",                main = "Ellipsoid 1")  plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse2,                background = back_data[, ref_ellipse2$var_names],                fixed_lims = global_lims,                col_ell = "#0004d5", col_bg = "#9a9797",                lwd = 2, pch = ".",                xlab = "Bio1", ylab = "Bio12",                main = "Ellipsoid 2 (shifted centroid)")

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: add_data() add_ellipsoid() layer content onto existing plot opened plot_ellipsoid(). key thing keep mind drawing order: whatever drawn last sits top. typical order background first, occurrence points second, ellipsoid boundary last.

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: common layering pattern. open plot muted background, add occurrence points distinctive color, draw ellipsoid boundary top obscured points.
code: # Simulate occurrence points from inside the ellipsoid set.seed(42) occ_idx <- sample(which(pred_trunc$suitability_trunc > 0), 40) occ_pts <- back_data[occ_idx, ref_ellipse$var_names]  par(mar = mars)  # Step 1: background (muted color, boundary also muted so it sits behind) plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                background = back_data[, ref_ellipse$var_names],                col_bg  = "#c5c5c5",                col_ell = "#c5c5c5",                pch = ".", lwd = 1,                xlab = "Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature)",                ylab = "Bio12 (Annual Precipitation)",                main = "Background + occurrences + boundary")  # Step 2: occurrence points on top of background add_data(occ_pts,          x = "bio_1", y = "bio_12",          pts_col = "#0004d5", pch = 19, cex = 1.2)  # Step 3: ellipsoid boundary on top of everything add_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse, col_ell = "#e10000", lwd = 2)  legend("topright",        legend = c("Background", "Occurrences", "Ellipsoid boundary"),        col    = c("#c5c5c5", "#0004d5", "#e10000"),        pch    = c(16, 19, NA), lty = c(NA, NA, 1),        lwd    = c(NA, NA, 2), cex = 0.8, bty = "n")

what: Coloring occurrence points by a continuous variable
title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: add_data() also supports col_layer, maps continuous column onto palette way plot_ellipsoid() . useful showing suitability, Mahalanobis distance, variable occurrence locations top background.
code: # Predict suitability at the occurrence points occ_pred <- predict(ref_ellipse,                     newdata = occ_pts,                     include_suitability = TRUE,                     include_mahalanobis = FALSE,                     verbose = FALSE)  par(mar = mars)  # Background in grey plot_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse,                background = back_data[, ref_ellipse$var_names],                col_bg  = "#d4d4d4",                col_ell = "#e10000",                pch = ".", lwd = 2,                xlab = "Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature)",                ylab = "Bio12 (Annual Precipitation)",                main = "Occurrences colored by suitability")  # Occurrence points colored by suitability add_data(occ_pred,          x = "bio_1", y = "bio_12",          col_layer = "suitability",          pal       = vir_pal,          pch = 19, cex = 1.4)  add_ellipsoid(ref_ellipse, col_ell = "#e10000", lwd = 2)  legend("topright",        legend = c("Background", "Ellipsoid boundary",                   "Low suitability", "High suitability"),        col    = c("#d4d4d4", "#e10000", vir_pal[5], vir_pal[95]),        pch    = c(16, NA, 19, 19), lty = c(NA, 1, NA, NA),        lwd    = c(NA, 2, NA, NA), cex = 0.8, bty = "n")

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: plot_ellipsoid_pairs() automatically generates pairwise two-dimensional projections ellipsoid arranges grid. number panels (p2)\binom{p}{2} pp number dimensions. two-dimensional ellipsoid one pair; three dimensions three; . key feature axis limits pre-computed globally across variables shared across panels. Without , panel rescale data extent, making niche widths appear identical across dimensions even differ substantially.

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: panel shows ellipsoid projected onto different pair variables. axis limits shared globally, relative spread ellipsoid across dimensions visually preserved. empty cell 2x2 grid consequence three pairs layout rounds four cells.
code: # Build a 3D ellipsoid for a more interesting pairs example range_3d <- data.frame(bio_1  = c(27, 35),                        bio_12 = c(1000, 1500),                        bio_15 = c(60, 75))  ellipse_3d <- build_ellipsoid(range = range_3d) #> Starting: building ellipsoidal niche from ranges... #> Step: computing covariance matrix... #> Step: computing additional ellipsoidal niche metrics... #> Done: created ellipsoidal niche.  par(cex = 0.7)  plot_ellipsoid_pairs(ellipse_3d,                      background = back_data[, ellipse_3d$var_names],                      col_ell = "#e10000",                      col_bg  = "#9a9797",                      lwd = 2, pch = ".")

title: Visualizing ellipsoids in environmental space
text: plot_ellipsoid_pairs() also accepts prediction directly. recommended way show suitability Mahalanobis distance across pairwise projections .   grey outside region colored inside region behave identically single plot_ellipsoid() call truncated col_layer, applied consistently across every panel.
code: suit_3d <- predict(ellipse_3d,                    newdata = back_data[, ellipse_3d$var_names],                    include_mahalanobis   = FALSE,                    include_suitability   = TRUE,                    suitability_truncated = TRUE,                    verbose = FALSE)  par(cex = 0.7)  plot_ellipsoid_pairs(ellipse_3d,                      prediction = suit_3d,                      col_layer  = "suitability_trunc",                      pal        = vir_pal,                      col_bg     = "#d4d4d4",                      col_ell    = "#e10000",                      lwd = 2, pch = 16, cex_bg = 0.3) par(original_par)

title: Predicting suitability and Mahalanobis distance
text: ellipsoidal niche model nicheR represents species’ niche region multivariate environmental space. ellipsoid’s shape comes covariance structure environmental conditions user provides, center, centroid, favorable environmental combination. Every prediction starts Mahalanobis distance (D2D^2), measures far environmental point 𝐱\mathbf{x} niche centroid 𝛍\boldsymbol{\mu}: D2=(𝐱−𝛍)⊤𝚺−1(𝐱−𝛍)D^2 = (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) 𝚺−1\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} inverse covariance matrix. simpler terms, D2D^2 measures unusual set environmental conditions relative niche center, accounting differences variable scale correlations among variables. Unlike Euclidean distance, stretches rotates shape niche. ellipsoid surface E-space exactly set points constant Mahalanobis distance centroid. smaller D2D^2 means environmental conditions similar niche center. larger D2D^2 means increasingly different. Critically, D2D^2 bounded 0 1: distance, probability.

what: From distance to suitability: the chi-square and MVN connection
title: Predicting suitability and Mahalanobis distance
text: nicheR converts D2D^2 suitability using multivariate normal (MVN) kernel: S=exp(−12D2)S = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2} D^2\right) transformation arbitrary. exponent MVN probability density function exactly −12D2-\frac{1}{2} D^2, suitability proportional MVN density 𝐱\mathbf{x}. words, treating niche multivariate Gaussian centered 𝛍\boldsymbol{\mu} covariance 𝚺\boldsymbol{\Sigma}, suitability relative likelihood species occurring conditions 𝐱\mathbf{x}. rescales distance value 0 1 intuitive ecologically: 1 centroid, declining smoothly toward 0 conditions move away optimum. connection MVN also determines ellipsoid boundary. MVN assumption, D2D^2 follows chi-square distribution pp degrees freedom, pp number environmental variables. confidence level cl stored ellipsoid corresponds χp,cl2\chi^2_{p,\,\text{cl}} quantile: ellipsoid surface set points D2=χp,cl2D^2 = \chi^2_{p,\,\text{cl}}, enclosing fraction cl MVN probability mass. Setting cl = 0.95 means ellipsoid contains 95% theoretical MVN density. figure brings ideas together. top row shows E-space scatter colored D2D^2 histogram D2D^2 values across background. bottom row shows suitability. vertical dashed lines histograms mark chi-square quantiles three probability levels, showing exactly ellipsoid boundary falls relative distribution background distances.   color gradients scatter plots reversed two metrics, reflecting mathematical relationship: D2D^2 increases move away centroid, darker colors near center indicate lower distance. Suitability decreases distance, brighter colors near center indicate higher suitability. Distance measures far point niche center; suitability transforms distance ecologically interpretable value 0 1. histograms connect MVN. MVN assumption, D2D^2 follows chi-square distribution, vertical dashed lines mark chi-square quantiles 50%, 90%, 99%, correspond ellipsoid boundaries confidence levels. thresholds appear suitability histogram, showing equivalent suitability value boundary. makes clear choice cl building ellipsoid direct interpretable effect suitability value marks edge niche.

title: Predicting suitability and Mahalanobis distance
text: Beyond basic call, predict() lets control outputs returned independently. four output types : Two additional arguments control returned alongside predictions: keep_data: whether predictor columns included returned object. Default TRUE data.frame input FALSE SpatRaster. Coordinate columns (x, y, lon, lat, etc.) always retained keep_data = TRUE. adjust_truncation_level: number strictly 0 1 overrides cl stored ellipsoid truncated outputs, without refitting ellipsoid. See Effect confidence level predictions.

title: Predicting suitability and Mahalanobis distance

what: The role of the confidence level and truncation
title: Predicting suitability and Mahalanobis distance
text: ellipsoid boundary defined chi-square cutoff χp,cl2\chi^2_{p,\,\text{cl}}. Without truncation, D2D^2 SS computed every point newdata regardless whether falls inside outside ellipsoid, every point Earth receives positive suitability value. can misleading goal identify species potentially occur. Truncation operationalizes ellipsoid boundary ecologically: mahalanobis_truncated = TRUE: points D2>χp,cl2D^2 > \chi^2_{p,\,\text{cl}} receive NA, meaning environments outside niche distance reported. suitability_truncated = TRUE: points D2>χp,cl2D^2 > \chi^2_{p,\,\text{cl}} receive S=0S = 0, meaning unsuitable environments explicitly scored zero rather receiving small positive value. plots Truncated predictions E-space Truncated suitability map show truncated outputs. Outside points rendered grey full background extent preserved view.

what: Effect of the confidence level on predictions
title: Predicting suitability and Mahalanobis distance
text: choice cl directly controls much environmental space classified suitable. lower cl produces smaller, conservative ellipsoid boundary; higher cl produces larger, permissive one. ellipsoid object remains unchanged, boundary used truncation shifts. adjust_truncation_level argument lets explore different thresholds without refitting ellipsoid: adjust_truncation_level must single number strictly 0 1 affects truncated outputs. figure shows truncated suitability E-space three confidence levels side side.   cl increases 0.90 0.99 suitable area grows larger ellipsoid object remains unchanged. colored interior represents gradient suitability within boundary grey points classified outside. choice cl meaningful modeling decision justified context research question.

title: Sampling occurrence data with bias
text: nicheR package provides flexible suite sampling functions drawing occurrence points raster layers. vignette covers workflow simulating spatially biased occurrence data: Defining fundamental niche space (build_ellipsoid()). Projecting niche geographic space (predict()). Simulating biased sampling efforts reflect real-world uneven data collection (sample_biased_data()).

title: Sampling occurrence data with bias
text: First, load core packages required spatial niche operations.
code: # Load packages library(nicheR) library(terra)

title: Sampling occurrence data with bias
text: build_ellipsoid() begin defining fundamental niche species based absolute environmental tolerances.
code: # Load environmental covariates (Bio1, Bio12, Bio15) bios_file <- system.file("extdata", "ma_bios.tif", package = "nicheR") bios <- rast(bios_file) vars <- c("bio_1", "bio_12", "bio_15")  # Define environmental ranges (Min and Max) range_df <- data.frame(bio_1  = c(22, 28),                        bio_12 = c(1000, 3500),                        bio_15 = c(50, 70))  # Build ellipsoid niche model ell <- build_ellipsoid(range = range_df) #> Starting: building ellipsoidal niche from ranges... #> Step: computing covariance matrix... #> Step: computing additional ellipsoidal niche metrics... #> Done: created ellipsoidal niche.

title: Sampling occurrence data with bias
text: predict() niche space defined, project onto geographic landscape (SpatRaster environmental variables). yields spatial predictions habitat suitability environmental distance (Mahalanobis distance).

title: Sampling occurrence data with bias

title: Sampling occurrence data with bias
text: sample_bias_data() Objective: simulate spatially biased occurrence data prediction layer. Purpose: Real-world biodiversity data rarely collected systematically. Biologists often sample near roads, rivers, research stations. workflow allows simulate spatial sampling bias test robust ecological models imperfect detection clustered collection efforts.

title: Sampling occurrence data with bias
text: prepare_bias(): Standardizes raw bias layer (e.g., distance roads) probability surface. apply_bias(): Multiplies underlying prediction surface (suitability distance) prepared bias surface. sample_biased_data(): Draws actual points newly integrated biased surface.

title: Sampling occurrence data with bias
text: prepare_bias() applying spatial bias ecological predictions, must convert raw bias proxies (e.g., distance roads, human population density) standardized probability surfaces. prepare_bias() function standardizes one raw raster layers (min-max normalization 0-1 scale) allows define layer restricts sampling. multiple layers provided, multiplies together create single composite bias surface.

previous_headings: Introducing Sampling Bias > Preparing the Bias Layer
title: Sampling occurrence data with bias
text: bias_surface: SpatRaster (single multi-layer) list SpatRaster objects representing raw bias covariates. "direct": Higher values raster increase sampling probability. "inverse": Higher values raster decrease sampling probability (automatically transformed 1 - value). Note: providing multiple bias layers, can vector mapping direction respective layer. template_layer: Optional. reference SpatRaster used align bias layers. NULL, finest-resolution bias layer used automatically. include_composite: Logical. TRUE (default), returns final multiplied composite bias surface. include_processed_layers: Logical. TRUE, returns standardized individual layers multiplied together. Default FALSE. mask_na: Logical. Controls NA values combined. TRUE, pixel NA layer becomes NA composite (intersection). FALSE (default), uses union extents ignores NAs layers valid values. verbose: Logical. Prints progress messages TRUE.
code: # Load the raw bias layer(s) biases_file <- system.file("extdata", "ma_biases.tif", package = "nicheR") biases <- rast(biases_file)  # Prepare bias surfaces  # Assuming 'biases' has two layers, we map a direct effect to the first  # and an inverse effect to the second. prep_biases <- prepare_bias(   bias_surface = biases,   effect_direction = c("direct", "inverse"),   include_composite = TRUE,   verbose = TRUE ) #> Starting: prepare_bias() #> Step: splitting SpatRaster into layers... #> Step: bias_surface is a SpatRaster. Using first layer as template surface... #> Step: mask_na = FALSE. Expanding template to union extent (keeping finest resolution). #> Step: standarizing (min/max) and applying direction of effect to 2 bias layer/s... #> Step: building standarized (min/max) directional composite bias surface (mask_na = FALSE)... #> Done: prepare_bias()

title: Sampling occurrence data with bias
text: apply_bias() composite bias surface prepared, must integrated habitat suitability environmental distance maps. apply_bias() function mathematically merges niche prediction bias surface, aligning grids necessary, cropping output exact domain suitability map.

previous_headings: Introducing Sampling Bias > Applying Bias to Predictions
title: Sampling occurrence data with bias
text: prepared_bias: output list prepare_bias(), single-layer SpatRaster composite bias surface. prediction: SpatRaster containing environmental predictions (suitability Mahalanobis distance) generated predict(). Values must scaled 0 1. prediction_layer: Character. specific layer name extract prediction object (e.g., "suitability", "Mahalanobis_trunc"). effect_direction: Character. Dictates bias multiplied prediction. "direct" (default): Multiplies prediction bias directly. "inverse": Multiplies prediction (1 - bias). verbose: Logical. Prints progress messages TRUE.
code: # 1. Suitability applied with Direct Bias app_suit_dir <- apply_bias(   prepared_bias = prep_biases,    prediction = pred,    prediction_layer = "suitability",    effect_direction = "direct" ) #> Starting: apply_bias() #> Step: applying bias with 'direct' effect to to "suitability" layer... #> Done: apply_bias(). Note: values are no longer probabilities  # 2. Truncated Suitability applied with Inverse Bias app_suit_trunc_inv <- apply_bias(   prepared_bias = prep_biases,    prediction = pred,    prediction_layer = "suitability_trunc",    effect_direction = "inverse" ) #> Starting: apply_bias() #> Step: applying bias with 'inverse' effect to to "suitability_trunc" layer... #> Done: apply_bias(). Note: values are no longer probabilities  # 3. Mahalanobis Distance applied with Direct Bias app_maha_dir <- apply_bias(   prepared_bias = prep_biases,    prediction = pred,    prediction_layer = "Mahalanobis",    effect_direction = "direct" ) #> Starting: apply_bias() #> Step: applying bias with 'direct' effect to to "Mahalanobis" layer... #> Done: apply_bias(). Note: values are no longer probabilities  # 4. Truncated Mahalanobis Distance applied with Inverse Bias app_maha_trunc_inv <- apply_bias(   prepared_bias = prep_biases,    prediction = pred,    prediction_layer = "Mahalanobis_trunc",    effect_direction = "inverse" ) #> Starting: apply_bias() #> Step: applying bias with 'inverse' effect to to "Mahalanobis_trunc" layer... #> Done: apply_bias(). Note: values are no longer probabilities

code: # Scenario 1: Suitability | Direct Bias | Strict = FALSE occ_suit_dir <- sample_biased_data(n_occ = 100,                                     prediction = app_suit_dir,                                     prediction_layer = "suitability_biased_direct",                                     strict = FALSE) #> Starting: sample_biased_data() #> Done: sampled 100 points from biased prediction layer env_suit_dir <- terra::extract(bios, occ_suit_dir[, c(1:2)])  # Scenario 2: Suitability Truncated | Inverse Bias | Strict = TRUE occ_suit_trunc_inv <- sample_biased_data(n_occ = 100,                                           prediction = app_suit_trunc_inv,                                           prediction_layer = "suitability_trunc_biased_inverse",                                          strict = TRUE) #> Starting: sample_biased_data() #>  #> Done: sampled 100 points from biased prediction layer env_suit_trunc_inv <- terra::extract(bios, occ_suit_trunc_inv[, c(1:2)])  # Scenario 3: Mahalanobis | Direct Bias | Strict = FALSE occ_maha_dir <- sample_biased_data(n_occ = 100,                                    prediction = app_maha_dir,                                     prediction_layer = "Mahalanobis_biased_direct",                                    strict = FALSE) #> Starting: sample_biased_data() #>  #> Done: sampled 100 points from biased prediction layer env_maha_dir <- terra::extract(bios, occ_maha_dir[, c(1:2)])  # Scenario 4: Mahalanobis Truncated | Inverse Bias | Strict = TRUE occ_maha_trunc_inv <- sample_biased_data(n_occ = 100,                                          prediction = app_maha_trunc_inv,                                           prediction_layer = "Mahalanobis_trunc_biased_inverse",                                           strict = TRUE) #> Starting: sample_biased_data() #>  #> Done: sampled 100 points from biased prediction layer env_maha_trunc_inv <- terra::extract(bios, occ_maha_trunc_inv[, c(1:2)])

title: Sampling occurrence data
text: nicheR package provides flexible suite sampling functions drawing occurrence points raster layers, data frames, virtual species objects. vignette covers first three steps core workflow: Defining fundamental niche space environmental dimensions (build_ellipsoid()). Projecting niche geographic space generate prediction surface (predict()). Sampling standard occurrence points comparing distributions environmental space (e-space) geographic space (g-space) (sample_data()).

title: Sampling occurrence data
text: First, need load core packages required spatial niche operations.
code: # Load packages library(nicheR) library(terra)

title: Sampling occurrence data
text: build_ellipsoid() first step simulating modeling species distributions defining environmental conditions species can tolerate (fundamental niche). defining ranges environmental variables building niche ellipsoid.

title: Sampling occurrence data
text: range: data frame column represents environmental variable, rows contain minimum maximum tolerable values species.
code: # Load environmental covariates (Bio1, Bio12, Bio15) bios_file <- system.file("extdata", "ma_bios.tif", package = "nicheR") bios <- rast(bios_file) vars <- c("bio_1", "bio_12", "bio_15")  # Define environmental ranges (Min and Max) range_df <- data.frame(bio_1  = c(22, 28),                        bio_12 = c(1000, 3500),                        bio_15 = c(50, 70))  # Build ellipsoid niche model ell <- build_ellipsoid(range = range_df) #> Starting: building ellipsoidal niche from ranges... #> Step: computing covariance matrix... #> Step: computing additional ellipsoidal niche metrics... #> Done: created ellipsoidal niche.

title: Sampling occurrence data
text: predict() niche space defined, project onto geographic landscape (SpatRaster environmental variables). yields spatial predictions habitat suitability environmental distance (Mahalanobis distance).

title: Sampling occurrence data
text: object: ellipsoid object created previous step. newdata: spatial raster (SpatRaster) containing environmental layers geographic region. include_mahalanobis: Logical. TRUE, calculates Mahalanobis distance niche centroid pixel. include_suitability: Logical. TRUE, converts distances continuous habitat suitability index (0 1). suitability_truncated / mahalanobis_truncated: Logical. TRUE, truncates metrics areas strictly outside defined ellipsoid limits assigned suitability 0 (maximum distance Mahalanobis).
code: # Predict spatial surfaces based on the defined ellipsoid pred <- predict(ell,                 newdata = bios,                 include_mahalanobis = TRUE,                 include_suitability = TRUE,                 suitability_truncated = TRUE,                 mahalanobis_truncated = TRUE,                 keep_data = TRUE) #> Starting: suitability prediction using newdata of class: SpatRaster... #> Step: Ignoring extra predictor columns: bio_5, bio_6, bio_7, bio_13, bio_14 #> Step: Using 3 predictor variables: bio_1, bio_12, bio_15 #> Done: Prediction completed successfully. Returned raster layers: bio_1, bio_12, bio_15, Mahalanobis, suitability, Mahalanobis_trunc, suitability_trunc  # Visualize the resulting continuous prediction layers plot(pred)

title: Sampling occurrence data
text: sample_data() Objective: generate presence points continuous prediction surface (e.g., habitat suitability Mahalanobis distance) using weighted random sampling.

title: Sampling occurrence data
text: n_occ: total number occurrence points sample. prediction: multi-layer SpatRaster generated predict(). prediction_layer: specific layer name within raster use base sampling weights (e.g., "suitability", "Mahalanobis_trunc"). sampling: Determines spatial bias sampling probability: "centroid": Samples frequently optimal conditions. "edge": Samples frequently marginal conditions. "random": Samples uniformly based strictly available space. method: Two weighting methods control probability used draw samples: "suitability" — weights suitability score. "mahalanobis" — weights Mahalanobis distance centroid. strict: Logical. TRUE, algorithm strictly forbids sampling pixels outside fundamental niche boundaries.

title: Sampling occurrence data
text: , generate four separate occurrence datasets reflecting different theoretical sampling scenarios. also extract environmental data points can plot e-space later.
code: # Scenario A: Centroid Sampling from Suitability # Generates points strongly biased toward the absolute best habitat. # Suitability | Centroid | Strict = FALSE occ_suit_cent <- sample_data(   n_occ = 100,   prediction = pred,   prediction_layer = "suitability",   sampling = "centroid",   method = "suitability",   seed = 123,   strict = FALSE ) #> Starting: sample_data() #> Done: sampled 100 points.  # Scenario B: Edge Sampling from Mahalanobis Distance # Simulates a species frequently found in its marginal/edge environments. # Mahalanobis | Edge | Strict = FALSE occ_maha_edge <- sample_data(   n_occ = 100,   prediction = pred,   prediction_layer = "Mahalanobis",   sampling = "edge",   method = "mahalanobis",   seed = 123,   strict = FALSE ) #> Starting: sample_data() #>  #> Done: sampled 100 points.  # Scenario C: Random Sampling from Truncated Suitability # A proportional sample where points are restricted strictly to suitable areas. # Suitability Truncated | Random | Strict = TRUE occ_suit_trunc_rand <- sample_data(   n_occ = 100,   prediction = pred,   prediction_layer = "suitability_trunc",   sampling = "random",   method = "suitability",   seed = 123,   strict = TRUE ) #> Starting: sample_data() #>  #> Done: sampled 100 points.  # Scenario D: Edge Sampling from Truncated Mahalanobis Distance # Marginal sampling, but strictly forbidden from crossing the hard threshold. # Mahalanobis Truncated | Edge | Strict = TRUE occ_maha_trunc_edge <- sample_data(   n_occ = 100,   prediction = pred,   prediction_layer = "Mahalanobis_trunc",   sampling = "edge",   method = "mahalanobis",   seed = 123,   strict = TRUE ) #> Starting: sample_data() #>  #> Done: sampled 100 points.

title: Sampling occurrence data
text: E-Space represents n-dimensional coordinate system defined environmental variables. plotting sampled points build_ellipsoid() object, can see exactly sampled occurrences fall relative species’ fundamental niche tolerances. Notice "centroid" sampling clusters points near red square (optimal niche center), "edge" sampling pushes points outward toward borders ellipsoid.

text: G-Space represents physical, real-world landscape defined spatial coordinates (Longitude vs. Latitude). , plot exact four datasets top respective prediction maps. duality reveals clustering center E-Space translates points occurring highest suitability geographic patches, whereas E-Space edge sampling translates points scattered peripheral, marginal habitats map.
code: par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 2))   # Plot 1: Suitability | Centroid | Strict = FALSE plot(pred[["suitability"]], main = "Suitability | Centroid | Strict = FALSE", col = grDevices::terrain.colors(50)) points(occ_suit_cent[, c(1:2)], pch = 20, col = "black", cex = 1.2)  # Plot 2: Mahalanobis | Edge | Strict = FALSE plot(pred[["Mahalanobis"]], main = "Mahalanobis | Edge | Strict = FALSE", col = rev(grDevices::terrain.colors(50))) points(occ_maha_edge[, c(1:2)], pch = 20, col = "black", cex = 1.2)  # Plot 3: Truncated Suitability | Random | Strict = TRUE plot(pred[["suitability_trunc"]], main = "Suitability Truncated | Random | Strict = TRUE", col = grDevices::terrain.colors(50)) points(occ_suit_trunc_rand[, c(1:2)], pch = 20, col = "black", cex = 1.2)  # Plot 4: Truncated Mahalanobis | Edge | Strict = TRUE plot(pred[["Mahalanobis_trunc"]], main = "Mahalanobis Truncated | Edge | Strict = TRUE", col = rev(grDevices::terrain.colors(50))) points(occ_maha_trunc_edge[, c(1:2)], pch = 20, col = "black", cex = 1.2) dev.off() #> null device  #>           1

title: Sampling occurrence data with virtual data
text: Virtual species highly useful controlled, purely theoretical experiments quantitative ecology. vignette covers workflow simulating virtual species data entirely within environmental space (E-Space). bypasses need geographic rasters, allowing generate sample occurrence points directly mathematical properties defined niche ellipsoid.

title: Sampling occurrence data with virtual data
text: First, load core packages required operations.
code: # Load packages library(nicheR) library(terra)

title: Sampling occurrence data with virtual data
text: define fundamental niche based absolute environmental tolerances.
code: # Define environmental ranges (Min and Max) range_df <- data.frame(bio_1  = c(22, 28),                        bio_12 = c(1000, 3500),                        bio_15 = c(50, 70))  # Build ellipsoid niche model ell <- build_ellipsoid(range = range_df) #> Starting: building ellipsoidal niche from ranges... #> Step: computing covariance matrix... #> Step: computing additional ellipsoidal niche metrics... #> Done: created ellipsoidal niche.

title: Sampling occurrence data with virtual data
text: virtual_data() Instead applying niche geographic map, simulate points drawn mathematical distribution niche .

title: Sampling occurrence data with virtual data
text: object: nicheR_ellipsoid object. n: number virtual points generate within environmental space. truncate: Logical. TRUE, restricts generated points strictly fall within ellipsoid’s confidence limits. FALSE, points drawn standard multivariate normal distribution. effect: Dictates point distribution pattern. "direct": Clusters points near optimal centroid. "inverse": Pushes points toward marginal edges. "uniform": Distributes points evenly throughout volume (requires truncate = TRUE).
code: # Simulate 1,000 background virtual data points clustered near the center virt_dat <- virtual_data(ell,                          n = 1000,                          truncate = FALSE,                          effect = "direct",                          seed = 1)

title: Sampling occurrence data with virtual data
text: Now score simulated virtual data points calculate true habitat suitability Mahalanobis distance. Notice pass data frame (virt_dat) newdata argument instead raster.
code: # Predict suitability directly onto the virtual data frame pred <- predict(ell,                 newdata = virt_dat,                 include_mahalanobis = TRUE,                 include_suitability = TRUE,                 suitability_truncated = TRUE,                 mahalanobis_truncated = TRUE,                 keep_data = TRUE) # Keeps the environmental variables attached #> Starting: suitability prediction using newdata of class: matrix, array... #> Step: Using 3 predictor variables: bio_1, bio_12, bio_15 #> Done: Prediction completed successfully. Returned columns: bio_1, bio_12, bio_15, Mahalanobis, suitability, Mahalanobis_trunc, suitability_trunc

title: Sampling occurrence data with virtual data
text: sample_virtual_data() Objective: sample virtual occurrence points non-spatial prediction data frame. function acts data-frame equivalent sample_data(). inherently supports weighting rules ("centroid", "edge", "random") methods ("suitability", "mahalanobis").

title: Sampling occurrence data with virtual data
text: virtual_prediction: data frame output predict() function. prediction_layer: name column containing prediction metric use sampling weight. sampling: Spatial bias sampling probability ("centroid", "edge", "random"). method: Weighting interpretation ("suitability" "mahalanobis"). strict: Logical. TRUE, removes zero-valued rows sampling. NULL, auto-detects based column name presence zeros/NAs.

title: Sampling occurrence data with virtual data
code: # Scenario 1: Suitability | Centroid | Strict = FALSE occ_virt_suit_cent <- sample_virtual_data(n_occ = 100,                                            object = ell,                                            virtual_prediction = pred, prediction_layer = "suitability",                                            sampling = "centroid",                                            method = "suitability",                                            seed = 123) #> Starting: sample_virtual_data() #> Warning in resolve_prediction(virtual_prediction, prediction_layer): #> 'prediction' is a data.frame, and it is missing 'x' and 'y', results wont show #> geographical connections. #> Done: sampled 100 points.  # Scenario 2: Suitability Truncated | Random | Strict = TRUE occ_virt_suit_trunc_rand <- sample_virtual_data(n_occ = 100,                                                  object = ell,                                                  virtual_prediction = pred,                                                  prediction_layer = "suitability_trunc",                                                  sampling = "random",                                                 method = "suitability",                                                  strict = TRUE,                                                  seed = 123) #> Starting: sample_virtual_data() #> Warning in resolve_prediction(virtual_prediction, prediction_layer): #> 'prediction' is a data.frame, and it is missing 'x' and 'y', results wont show #> geographical connections. #> Done: sampled 100 points.  # Scenario 3: Mahalanobis | Edge | Strict = FALSE occ_virt_maha_edge <- sample_virtual_data(n_occ = 100,                                            object = ell,                                            virtual_prediction = pred, prediction_layer = "Mahalanobis",                                            sampling = "edge",                                            method = "mahalanobis",                                           seed = 123) #> Starting: sample_virtual_data() #> Warning in resolve_prediction(virtual_prediction, prediction_layer): #> 'prediction' is a data.frame, and it is missing 'x' and 'y', results wont show #> geographical connections. #> Done: sampled 100 points.  # Scenario 4: Mahalanobis Truncated | Centroid | Strict = TRUE occ_virt_maha_trunc_cent <- sample_virtual_data(n_occ = 100,                                                  object = ell,                                                  virtual_prediction = pred, prediction_layer = "Mahalanobis_trunc",                                                  sampling = "centroid",                                                 method = "mahalanobis",                                                  strict = TRUE,                                                  seed = 123) #> Starting: sample_virtual_data() #> Warning in resolve_prediction(virtual_prediction, prediction_layer): #> 'prediction' is a data.frame, and it is missing 'x' and 'y', results wont show #> geographical connections. #> Done: sampled 100 points.

code: # Simulating the community with the full background rand_comm_full <- random_ellipses(object = ref_ellipse,                                   background = back_data[, vars], n = 25)  # Simulating the community with a thinned background rand_comm_thin <- random_ellipses(object = ref_ellipse,                                   background = back_data[, vars], n = 25,                                   thin_background = TRUE, resolution = 20) # Plotting the communities par(mfrow = c(1, 2), cex = 0.6, mar = mars)  # set up the plotting area  ## Plotting the community with the full background plot_community(rand_comm_full, background = back_data[, vars],                pch = ".", col_bg = "#9a9797",                xlab = "Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature)",                ylab = "Bio12 (Annual Precipitation)",                main = "Random Community from Full Background")  ## Plotting the community with the thinned background plot_community(rand_comm_thin, background = back_data[, vars],                pch = ".", col_bg = "#9a9797",                xlab = "Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature)",                ylab = "Bio12 (Annual Precipitation)",                main = "Random Community from Thinned Background")

title: Virtual community simulation

text: Bias layers represent external factors influence sampling detectability effort, independent species’ actual niche. layer assigned direction: "direct" means higher values increase sampling probability (e.g., higher species richness → likely good habitat), "inverse" means higher values decrease (e.g., higher light pollution → urbanization → less likely good habitat). prepare_bias() standardizes layers [0, 1], applies directional transformations, combines single composite bias surface. Setting include_processed_layers = TRUE returns standardized individual layers alongside composite, useful inspecting layer contributed.   Vignette: detailed walkthrough bias surface construction, directional effects, bias interacts predictions sampling, see: [insert link later Bias: preparation, prediction, sampling]
code: bias <- prepare_bias(bias_surface             = terra::rast(system.file("extdata/ma_biases.tif", package = "nicheR")),                      effect_direction         = c("direct", "inverse"),                      include_processed_layers = TRUE) #> Starting: prepare_bias() #> Step: splitting SpatRaster into layers... #> Step: bias_surface is a SpatRaster. Three sampling strategies control within niche occurrences drawn : "centroid" — samples preferentially near niche center (suitable conditions) "edge" — samples preferentially near niche boundary (marginal conditions) "random" — samples uniformly across suitable area Two weighting methods control probability used draw samples: "suitability" — weights suitability score "mahalanobis" — weights Mahalanobis distance centroid Using truncated prediction layer strict = TRUE ensures sampled points fall strictly within ellipsoid boundary.

title: Build a probabilistic ellipsoidal niche from ranges — build_ellipsoid
text: range must 2-row matrix data.frame variables columns. Rows represent lower upper bounds variable (row order may min/max max/min). centroid computed : $$\mu_i = (m_i + M_i)/2.$$ Marginal standard deviations derived assuming bounds represent approximately \(\pm 3\) standard deviations: $$\sigma_i = (M_i - m_i)/6,$$ diagonal covariance matrix assumed: $$\Sigma = \mathrm{diag}(\sigma_1^2,\dots,\sigma_n^2).$$ ellipsoid contour defined using chi-square cutoff \(c^2 = \chi^2_{n}(\mathrm{cl})\), \(n\) number variables.

title: Calculate n-dimensional ellipsoid metrics — ellipsoid_calculator
text: cov_matrix square, numeric covariance matrix \(\Sigma\). Must SPD. Row/column names (provided) used variable names output. centroid Numeric vector giving centroid \(\mu\). Must length equal ncol(cov_matrix). cl Numeric confidence level (0, 1). Used compute chi-square cutoff defining ellipsoid contour. verbose Logical; TRUE, prints progress messages.

title: Calculate n-dimensional ellipsoid metrics — ellipsoid_calculator
text: object class "nicheR_ellipsoid" created new_nicheR_ellipsoid, containing ellipsoid geometry associated quantities (e.g., centroid, covariance matrix, chi-square cutoff, semi-axis lengths, axis vertex coordinates, volume, covariance limits).

title: Calculate n-dimensional ellipsoid metrics — ellipsoid_calculator
text: ellipsoid boundary defined constant Mahalanobis distance contour: $$(x - \mu)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu) = c^2,$$ \(\mu\) centroid, \(\Sigma\) covariance matrix, \(c^2 = \chi^2_{n}(\mathrm{cl})\) chi-square cutoff \(n\) degrees freedom. covariance matrix must symmetric positive definite (SPD). inverse covariance computed via Cholesky factorization. Semi-axis lengths computed covariance eigenvalues \(\lambda_i\) : $$a_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i c^2}.$$ Axis vertices computed along eigenvector direction \(\mu \pm a_i v_i\). Hypervolume computed ellipsoid_volume, covariance-derived limits covariance_limits.

title: Generate data based on a ellipsoidal niche — virtual_data
text: object nicheR_ellipsoid object containing least centroid cov_matrix. n Integer. number virtual points generate. Default = 100. truncate Logical. TRUE (default), points constrained within confidence limit (cl) defined object. effect Character. distribution pattern points. "direct" (default) creates concentration near centroid. "inverse" creates higher density towards edges. "uniform" distributes points evenly throughout ellipsoid volume. Note: "inverse" "uniform" require truncate = TRUE. seed Integer. Random seed reproducibility. Default = 1. Set NULL seeding.

title: Generate data based on a ellipsoidal niche — virtual_data
text: matrix n rows columns corresponding environmental variables (dimensions) input object.

title: Generate data based on a ellipsoidal niche — virtual_data
text: truncate = FALSE, function generates points standard multivariate normal distribution defined ellipsoid's centroid covariance matrix, without constraints location. function uses eigen-decomposition transform standard normal variables coordinate system defined ellipsoid's covariance structure. truncate = TRUE, function generates candidate points uniformly distributed within bounding box (hyper-cube) defined ellipsoid's axes_coordinates. Points falling outside ellipsoid (Mahalanobis distance \(Md >\) chi2_cutoff) removed. filtered pool, n points selected using weighted random sampling without replacement. weights determined effect argument: "direct": Weights proportional multivariate normal   density (\(\exp(-0.5 \times Md)\)), clustering points near centroid. "inverse": Weights proportional complement   normal density (\(1 - \exp(-0.5 \times Md)\)), pushing points toward edges. "uniform": points within ellipsoid equal weight,   resulting uniform spatial distribution.
